subject: ophthalmology
title: Ophthalmology — Exam Notes
description: High-yield ophthalmology revision notes across 12 topics — cranial-nerve palsies, glaucoma, strabismus, retina, optic pathways, cornea, conjunctiva, lids & lacrimal, refraction, cataract, orbit/uvea and anatomy — plus reported exam topics.

sections.title: Cranial nerve palsies & diplopia
sections.html:
CN VI (abducens) palsy 2022

  • Lateral rectus paralysed → diplopia maximal in extreme gaze toward the affected side
  • Most common cause of sudden isolated diplopia in the elderly (microvascular — DM/HTN) [2022 Q1, Q52]

CN III (oculomotor) palsy 2022 2023

  • Signs: ptosis + mydriasis + 'down-and-out' eye (divergent, NOT convergent) [2022 Q22]
  • Recall: sudden-onset diplopia in a 75-year-old → 3rd nerve palsy (context-dependent; CN VI also commonly cited)
  • 2023 Q11: mydriasis is a feature; 'absence of ptosis' is false — ptosis IS present

CN IV (trochlear) palsy 2022

  • Superior oblique paralysed → diplopia maximal in down-gaze toward the opposite side
  • Right CN IV palsy → worst diplopia looking down and to the left

CN VII (facial) palsy 2022 2023

  • Causes lagophthalmos → exposure keratopathy; priority is corneal protection with lubricants [2023 Q22]
  • Does NOT cause corneal hypoaesthesia (CN V), ptosis (CN III), or diplopia [2022 Q41]
  • Bell's phenomenon is protective — its absence worsens exposure keratitis

Paralytic strabismus 2022 Recalls

  • Diplopia worst in gaze toward the direction of the paretic muscle

sections.title: Glaucoma
sections.html:
Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • NOT caused by increased aqueous production — caused by impaired trabecular drainage [2023 Q6; Recall]
  • Gradual loss of optic nerve function + optic disc cupping; symptomatic only late
  • Causes a superior (upper) arcuate scotoma — inferior nerve fibres affected first

Acute angle-closure glaucoma 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Triad: conjunctival hyperaemia + corneal haze + pupil mid-dilated & fixed [2023 Q19]
  • Pupil does NOT constrict to light; severe deep pain + nausea
  • Mydriatics are contraindicated [2023 Q27]; the cornea is hazy (not clear) [2024 Q38]

Congenital glaucoma Recalls

  • Buphthalmos is characteristic of congenital glaucoma — childhood cataract does NOT cause it

Glaucoma medications 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Prostaglandin analogues (latanoprost): ↑ uveoscleral outflow via PGF2α; do NOT block β2 [2023 Pharm]
  • Side effects: iris hyperpigmentation + increased eyelash growth
  • Miotics (pilocarpine): increase aqueous drainage

Raised IOP — mechanism 2023

  • Most common cause = reduced drainage through the canal of Schlemm / trabecular meshwork [2023 Q50]
  • Aqueous is produced by the ciliary body, NOT the iris

sections.title: Amblyopia & strabismus
sections.html:
Amblyopia 2022 2024 Recalls

  • Reduced VA in childhood only; NOT corrected in adolescence; treat early
  • Monocular occlusion in a 3-year-old causes amblyopia in ~3 days
  • Ptosis covering the pupil in a preschooler → treat immediately

Pseudo-strabismus (epicanthus) 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Apparent crossing from wide nasal bridge/epicanthus — no true deviation, requires no correction [2023 Q1]
  • Diagnosed by symmetric corneal light reflex (Hirschberg) / normal cover test

Accommodative convergent strabismus 2023 Recalls

  • High hyperopia in infants → over-accommodation → esotropia [2023 Q15]
  • Treated with glasses correcting the hyperopia [2023 Q43]

Cover-uncover test 2023

  • Exophoria: on covering, the covered eye drifts outward; on uncovering it moves back in to re-fixate [2023 Q17]
  • Phoria = latent · tropia = manifest

sections.title: Retinal conditions
sections.html:
CRAO & CRVO 2022 Recalls

  • CRAO: sudden, painless, near-total monocular loss; cherry-red spot; RAPD; more severe than CRVO
  • Ischaemic CRVO: sudden VA ≤1/20 + diffuse flame haemorrhages + cotton-wool spots; rubeosis is a complication [2022 Q48]
  • Branch RAO: sudden sectoral VA loss [2023 Q30]

Retinal detachment & PVD 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Types: rhegmatogenous, tractional, exudative — 'exogenous' is not a type
  • Floaters + photopsias + good VA → posterior vitreous detachment [2023 Q7; Recall]
  • Macular detachment → cannot have 10/10 vision
  • Vitrectomy NOT indicated for Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy

Diabetic retinopathy 2022 2024 Recalls

  • Earliest sign = microaneurysms
  • Proliferative DR = neovascularisation of retina/disc → vitreous haemorrhage, tractional detachment [2023 Q49]

AMD & macular disease 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • AMD: bilateral, normal IOP, gradual (dry) or rapid (wet) VA loss
  • Metamorphopsia = macular pathology → tested with the Amsler grid [2022 Q5; 2023 Q2]
  • Most urgent of a list: wet ARMD or stage-4 hypertensive retinopathy (swollen disc)

Retinoblastoma & ocular tumours 2022 2023 Recalls

  • Most common intraocular tumour in children; usually leukocoria; familial (~6%); before age 2 [2023 Q8]
  • Most common intraocular tumour in adults = choroidal (uveal) melanoma [2023 Q10]
  • GNA11 mutation → uveal melanoma (iris/choroid) [2023 Systemic Q1]

sections.title: Optic nerve & visual pathways
sections.html:
Retrobulbar (optic) neuritis & MS 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Hallmark: pain worsens with eye movement [2023 Q13; Recall]
  • RAPD present; disc looks normal acutely; associated with multiple sclerosis
  • Young/middle-aged patient with pain on eye movement → retrobulbar neuritis

Visual field defects 2022 2023

  • Full occipital lobe lesion → contralateral homonymous hemianopia (right lobe → left field) [2022 Q54; 2023 Q16]
  • Bitemporal hemianopia = chiasmal compression → temporal field scotomas in each eye [2023 Q28]
  • Superior arcuate scotoma = chronic glaucoma

Optic disc oedema 2022 2023

  • Pathological cupping = chronic glaucoma
  • AION (ischaemic): sudden significant vision loss
  • Papilloedema (raised ICP): venous congestion; early stage does NOT reduce VA [2022 Q55]

Giant cell arteritis (Horton's) 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Elderly + headache + jaw claudication + weight loss/malaise + raised ESR + risk of blindness (AION) [2022 Q57; 2023 Q9]
  • Treatment: urgent high-dose corticosteroids to save the other eye

sections.title: Cornea & keratitis
sections.html:
Herpetic (HSV) keratitis 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Dendritic ulcer; diagnosed with fluorescein + cobalt-blue light
  • NEVER use corticosteroids — converts to amoeboid ulcer, very hard to treat [Recall]
  • Unilateral, corneal hypoaesthesia, may scar; does cause pain ('not painful' is false)
  • HSV-1; latent in trigeminal ganglion [2023 Biopath Q3]

Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) 2022 2024 Recalls

  • Key feature: painful vesicles on one side of the face (dermatomal); starts with burning + redness
  • Treatment: antivirals (aciclovir) ± topical steroids — NOT topical antibiotics
  • Often associated iridocyclitis + raised IOP; lesions are pseudodendrites (not always dendritic ulcers) [2023 Q46]

Actinic (UV / welder's) keratitis 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Welder home in the evening, severe pain + tearing + ↓VA; delayed 6–12 h; punctate keratitis [2022 Q30,59; 2023 Q20]
  • Never treat with repeated topical anaesthetics (corneal toxic); use lubricants/patching

Corneal anatomy & transparency 2023 2024

  • Transparency maintained by the endothelium (Na⁺/K⁺ pump) keeping stroma dehydrated [2023 Q18]
  • Corneal endothelium does NOT regenerate
  • Ulcer diagnosis: fluorescein + cobalt-blue light [2023 Q12]

Neurotrophic keratopathy 2022 2023

  • Defined by reduced corneal sensation (not pain/itch) [2023 Q45]
  • Caused by HSV/HZV, CN V damage, or topical anaesthetic (tetracaine) abuse
  • Progresses: poor protection → thinning → perforation [2022 Q36]

Chemical burns 2023 2024

  • Lime/alkali splash → immediate copious irrigation + eyelid eversion + urgent referral [2023 Q47]
  • Alkali worse than acid (deeper penetration)

sections.title: Conjunctivitis
sections.html:
Type differentiation 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Purulent → bacterial · itchy + watery → allergic · epidemic + pharyngitis → viral (adenovirus)
  • Chronic, no mucopurulent discharge: children → chlamydial · elderly → dry eye

Viral conjunctivitis 2022 2023 2024

  • Adenoviral EKC contagious the first 2 weeks; main route is contact (not haematogenous)
  • Acute haemorrhagic keratoconjunctivitis: Coxsackie A24 / enterovirus 70 / adenovirus 11 [2023 Biopath Q2]

Neonatal ophthalmia 2022 2023

  • Causes: gonococcus, chlamydia, HSV, other agents; needs treatment (not self-limiting) [2023 Q3]
  • Unilateral mucous discharge in a neonate → nasolacrimal duct obstruction [2022 Q6]

sections.title: Eyelid & lacrimal
sections.html:
Entropion vs ectropion 2022 Recalls

  • Entropion: lid turns IN → lashes abrade cornea; NOT a complication of uveitis or thyroid disease
  • Ectropion: lid turns OUT → exposure + dryness [2022 Q10]

Chalazion vs hordeolum 2022 2023 Recalls

  • Chalazion: chronic meibomian lipogranuloma; warm compress, antibiotic if infected, I&C if persistent [2023 Q24]
  • Hordeolum (stye): acute staph; antibiotic drops + lid massage

Dacryocystitis 2022 2023

  • Acute: nasolacrimal duct obstruction → sac infection → systemic antibiotics [2023 Q36]
  • Chronic complications: abscess, corneal ulcer, medial canthal cellulitis [2022 Q7]

Sympathetic supply Recalls

  • Sympathetic → Müller's muscle + dilator pupillae (dilation)
  • 'sympathetic–levator–constrict' is a WRONG pairing — levator is CN III, sympathetic dilates

sections.title: Refractive errors & asthenopia
sections.html:
Myopia 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Image in front of retina; difficulty with distance; biconcave (diverging) lenses
  • Far point = 1/D metres: −2D → 50 cm · −3D → 33 cm · −5D → 20 cm [2022 Q42,47,49; 2023 Q48]
  • Myopia is the opposite of hyperopia (not presbyopia)

Presbyopia 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Age-related loss of accommodation from lens stiffening; near-vision difficulty; convex lenses
  • Myopes need less presbyopic correction — myopia compensates
  • An emmetrope can still have presbyopia [2023 Q23]

Hyperopia & asthenopia 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Asthenopia (κοπιωπία): eye strain in uncorrected hypermetropes and latent strabismus; worse with near/computer work, end of day [2023 Q42; Recall]
  • Student with eye pain after studying → hyperopia (↑ accommodative effort) [2023 Q44]
  • Resolves with rest/sleep → does not need urgent intervention

sections.title: Cataract
sections.html:
Clinical features 2022 2023 2024

  • Senile cataract: progressive painless VA loss; no redness [2023 Q26]
  • Hypermature cataract can cause phacomorphic glaucoma

Cataract surgery 2022 2023 2024 Recalls

  • Indicated when it interferes with daily activities — not a fixed VA cut-off [2024 recall]
  • Most severe complication: endophthalmitis → refer immediately [2022 Q37]
  • Urgent removal: unilateral congenital cataract (amblyopia risk) [2022 Q38]

Red reflex loss 2022 2024

  • Caused by opacification of lens, cornea, or vitreous
  • Leukocoria + lost red reflex → total cataract or retinoblastoma [2022 Q24]

sections.title: Orbit, sclera, uvea & dry eye
sections.html:
Orbital cellulitis (post-septal) 2022 Recalls

  • Distinguishing sign: restricted globe motility + chemosis + lid swelling → IV antibiotics [2022 Q17; Recall]

Scleritis & episcleritis 2022 2023

  • Scleritis: deep boring pain, tender, does NOT blanch with phenylephrine, risk of perforation, needs steroids [2022 Q40; 2023 Q14]
  • Episcleritis: mild, young adults, usually idiopathic, blanches with phenylephrine

Anterior uveitis (iritis) 2022 Recalls

  • Miosis + photophobia + pain + ↓VA; relieved by cycloplegics (atropine) [2022 Q50; Recall]

Thyroid eye disease 2022 2023 Recalls

  • Bilateral asymmetric exophthalmos, lid retraction, exposure keratopathy; does NOT reduce IOP or cause entropion [2023 Q32]
  • Compressive optic neuropathy: ↓VA + ↓colour vision + RAPD [2022 Q60]

Sjögren's & dry eye 2023 Recalls

  • Dry eyes + dry mouth; autoimmune; does NOT cause epiphora; Schirmer ≤5 mm [2023 Q31,40]
  • Damage is to lacrimal/salivary glands — not the meibomian glands

Pterygium & subconjunctival haemorrhage 2022 Recalls

  • Pterygium: grows from nasal side, induces astigmatism, treated surgically (not antibiotics) [2022 Q45]
  • Subconjunctival haemorrhage: self-limiting, no treatment if non-traumatic

Ciliary body 2021 2023

  • Contraction loosens zonules → lens rounds up for near focus (accommodation); produces aqueous humour [2023 Q29; 2021 Q53]

sections.title: Ocular anatomy & physiology
sections.html:
Chambers, uvea & sclera 2021

  • Anterior chamber = space between the posterior cornea and the anterior iris [2021 Q3]
  • Uvea = iris + ciliary body + choroid [2021 Q40]
  • Sclera: opaque, forms ~5/6 of the globe, supplied via episcleral ciliary arteries (the transparent one is the cornea) [2021 Q19]

Aqueous humour & ciliary body 2021 2023

  • Produced by the epithelium of the ciliary processes [2021 Q42]
  • Drained via the trabecular meshwork → canal of Schlemm (reduced drainage → raised IOP)
  • Ciliary-muscle contraction → zonules relax → lens rounds up for near (accommodation)

The near reflex & accommodation 2021

  • Near focus couples three things: accommodation + miosis + convergence [2021 Q1]
  • For near, lens dioptric power increases; presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation (lens stiffening)
  • A hyperope uses accommodation even for distance to pull the focus onto the retina [2021 Q50]

Cornea — transparency & layers 2021 2023 2024

  • Transparency maintained by the endothelial Na⁺/K⁺ pump keeping the stroma dehydrated [2021 Q37]
  • Corneal endothelium does not regenerate
  • Epithelial defect/ulcer → diagnose with fluorescein + cobalt-blue light

Horner's syndrome (Claude-Bernard-Horner) 2021

  • Triad: miosis + partial ptosis + facial anhidrosis (a sympathetic lesion) [2021 Q36]
  • Ptosis is from reduced Müller's-muscle action (not increased)
  • Congenital Horner's → iris heterochromia; the cause can be serious, so image/investigate

Marcus Gunn pupil (RAPD) 2021

  • An afferent (optic-nerve) defect found on the swinging-flashlight test
  • Affected eye: pupils paradoxically dilate when the light swings to it [2021 Q25]
  • Does not cause static anisocoria; not a CN III problem

Buphthalmos vs megalocornea 2021

  • Buphthalmos: enlarged cornea/globe (>12 mm) of congenital glaucoma — epiphora, corneal opacities, raised IOP [2021 Q16,30]
  • Megalocornea: usually clear and benign, normal vision

Ocular toxoplasmosis 2021

  • Causes posterior uveitis / focal retinochoroiditis with overlying vitritis ('headlight in the fog') [2021 Q39]
  • Can be acquired (not always congenital); treat when near the macula/disc — not always self-limiting

Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction 2021

  • Infant tearing + minimal discharge; most resolve with digital massage by ~12 months
  • If unresolved, probing of the duct around 12 months [2021 Q58]

Leading cause of low vision in the elderly 2021

  • In developed countries the commonest cause of reduced VA in people over 60 is age-related macular degeneration [2021 Q60]

sections.title: Reported exam topics (recalls)
sections.html:
July 2024 sitting — reported topics

Themes classmates remembered being tested

  • Glaucoma medication and primary open-angle glaucoma
  • Asthenopia (eye strain)
  • Strabismus — a child with left-eye esotropia
  • Binocular diplopia in a 75-year-old
  • "Painful loss of vision is NOT seen in…" — identify the painless condition

July 2024 — likely answers

Best-guess answers to the reported topics above

  • Glaucoma meds + POAG: first-line = prostaglandin analogue (e.g. latanoprost). POAG = gradual, painless optic-nerve damage with ↑IOP, not from increased aqueous production. PG side effects: iris hyperpigmentation + ↑ lash growth.
  • Asthenopia: uncorrected hypermetropes (extra accommodative effort); resolves with rest/glasses; no urgent intervention.
  • Child with left esotropia: likely accommodative esotropia → full hyperopic spectacle correction first; patch for amblyopia; always check the red reflex (exclude retinoblastoma/cataract).
  • Binocular diplopia, 75 y/o: acute ocular motor nerve palsy (CN III or VI); diplopia worst toward the paretic muscle. Pupil-involving CN III → urgent imaging (aneurysm); pupil-sparing → usually microvascular.
  • "Painful loss of vision is NOT seen in…": the painless option is the answer — CRAO, CRVO, amaurosis fugax, vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment are painless (painful = optic neuritis, AACG, GCA).

Confirmed answer points (recall list)

Treat these as settled facts

  • Post-op endophthalmitis is a cataract-surgery complication → refer immediately
  • Temporal bone is NOT the weakest orbital wall — the medial wall & floor are weakest
  • Floaters + flashing lights with good VA → posterior vitreous detachment
  • Corticosteroids do not cause ocular-motility complications
  • POAG is not due to increased aqueous production; manifests with gradual optic-nerve loss
  • Macular detachment → cannot have 10/10 vision
  • Prostaglandin side effects: iris hyperpigmentation + ↑ eyelash growth
  • Metamorphopsia → detected by Amsler grid
  • Pterygium is not treated with antibiotics (surgery if needed)
  • Entropion → corneal abrasion from lashes; not a complication of uveitis or thyroid disease
  • Herpetic keratitis → never cortisone (becomes amoeboid, hard to treat)
  • Sudden unilateral pain worse on movement → retrobulbar neuritis (also 30-year-old scenario)
  • Chalazion = meibomian-gland inflammation → warm compress, antibiotics if infected
  • Sudden-onset diplopia in a 75-year-old → 3rd nerve palsy (CN VI also commonly cited)
  • Paralytic strabismus → diplopia worst toward the paretic muscle's direction
  • Wrong pairing: sympathetic–levator–constrict
  • Asthenopia in hypermetropes who don't wear glasses
  • Proliferative DR → neovascularisation of retina/optic disc
  • Never wash contacts with tap water (Acanthamoeba risk)
  • Retinoblastoma familial transmission ~6%
  • Subconjunctival haemorrhage → no treatment when non-traumatic
  • Acute angle-closure glaucoma → pupil does not constrict to light (mid-dilated, fixed)
  • Malaise + headache + jaw claudication in an elderly patient → giant cell / temporal arteritis
  • CRAO is more severe than CRVO
  • Sjögren's does not cause epiphora (dry eyes, dry mouth)
  • Childhood cataract does not cause buphthalmos (that's congenital glaucoma)
  • HZV: redness, rash (lesions), pain
  • Anterior uveitis: miosis and photophobia
  • 67-year-old orbital cellulitis: chemosis, lid inflammation, restricted motility → IV antibiotics

Scenario-style recalls

Phrased as clinical vignettes / 'choose the odd one'

  • Needs more immediate treatment among drusen / dry-wet ARMD / stage-4 hypertensive retinopathy / vitreous detachment → wet ARMD or stage-4 hypertensive retinopathy (swollen disc)
  • Cataract treated surgically → when it interferes with daily activities
  • Initial feature of diabetic retinopathy → microaneurysms
  • Wrong about POAG → "caused by increased aqueous production"
  • 80-year-old, no presbyopia glasses for 2 years, blurry when driving → consider ARMD / secondary glaucoma (myopic shift)
  • Does not need urgent intervention → 20-year-old with end-of-day fatigue/asthenopia that resolves with sleep
  • NOT a cause of red-reflex loss → the distractor (lens, cornea & vitreous opacification all cause it)
  • NOT indicated for vitrectomy → Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy (vs RD, vitreous haemorrhage, macular hole)
  • False statement → "corneal endothelium regenerates" (it does not)
  • Chemical burn → irrigate with saline/water
  • Incorrect about viral conjunctivitis → "main route is haematogenous" (it's contact)
  • VZV key feature → painful vesicles on one side of the face
  • Eye pain after studying → hyperopia (↑ accommodative effort)
  • Retinal-detachment types → tractional, exudative, rhegmatogenous ('exogenous' is not real)

Image-based recalls

Photo identification items

  • Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis in a child — choose the incorrect statement
  • Pseudostrabismus (epicanthus) — approach: Hirschberg / cover test, reassure; no treatment
  • Convergent strabismus (esotropia) — choose the incorrect statement
